html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Five — Part Two

The Book of Iyov

IYOV

Wisdom — The Second and Third Cycles

Chapters 15 through 28

I Know That My Redeemer Lives — In My Flesh I Shall See Creator — Even Now My Witness Is in Heaven — Behold the Fear of the Creator That Is Wisdom

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QpaleoJobc — 11QtgJob Aramaic Targum

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

IYOV — THE SECOND AND THIRD CYCLES — CHAPTERS 15 THROUGH 28

IYOV — CHAPTER 15 Then Elifaz the Temanite Answered — Should a Wise Man Answer With Windy Knowledge — Are You the First Man Who Was Born — What Is Man That He Can Be Pure — The Wicked Man Writhes in Pain All His Days

1 Then Elifaz the Temanite answered and said:

2 Should a wise man answer with windy knowledge, and fill his belly with the east wind?

3 Should he argue in unprofitable talk, or in words with which he can do no good?

4 But you are doing away with the fear of Creator and hindering meditation before Creator.

5 For your iniquity teaches your mouth, and you choose the tongue of the crafty.

6 Your own mouth condemns you, and not I. Your own lips testify against you.

7 Are you the first man who was born? Or were you brought forth before the hills?

8 Have you listened in the council of Creator? And do you limit wisdom to yourself?

9 What do you know that we do not know? What do you understand that is not clear to us?

10 Both the gray-haired and the aged are among us, older than your father.

11 Are the consolations of Creator too small for you, or the word that deals gently with you?

12 Why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash,

13 That you turn your spirit against Creator and bring such words out of your mouth?

14 What is man, that he can be pure? Or he who is born of a woman, that he can be righteous?

15 Behold, Creator puts no trust in his holy ones, and the heavens are not pure in his sight.

16 How much less one who is abominable and corrupt, a man who drinks injustice like water.

17 I will show you. Hear me, and what I have seen I will declare,

18 What wise men have told, without hiding it, from their fathers,

19 To whom alone the land was given, and no stranger passed among them.

20 The wicked man writhes in pain all his days, through all the years that are laid up for the ruthless.

21 Dreadful sounds are in his ears. In prosperity the destroyer will come upon him.

22 He does not believe that he will return out of darkness, and he is marked for the sword.

23 He wanders abroad for bread, saying, Where is it? He knows that a day of darkness is ready at his hand.

24 Distress and anguish terrify him. They prevail against him, like a king ready for battle.

25 Because he has stretched out his hand against Creator and defies the Almighty,

26 Running stubbornly against him with a thickly bossed shield,

27 Because he has covered his face with his fat and gathered fat upon his waist

28 And has lived in desolate cities, in houses that none should inhabit, which were ready to become heaps of ruins,

29 He will not be rich, and his wealth will not endure, nor will his possessions spread over the earth.

30 He will not depart from darkness. The flame will dry up his shoots, and by the breath of his mouth he will depart.

31 Let him not trust in emptiness, deceiving himself, for emptiness will be his payment.

32 It will be paid in full before his time, and his branch will not be green.

33 He will shake off his unripe grape like the vine, and cast off his blossom like the olive tree.

34 For the company of the godless is barren, and fire consumes the tents of bribery.

35 They conceive trouble and give birth to evil, and their womb prepares deceit.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Iyov fragments confirm the text. The second cycle of the dialogue begins with Elifaz's second speech — his most aggressive. Where in Chapter 4-5 he spoke gently of the innocent not perishing, he now accuses Iyov directly of undermining the fear of Creator — verse 4 — and running stubbornly against the Almighty — verse 25-26. The movement from the friends' gentle first-cycle speeches to the harsh second-cycle speeches mirrors the hardening that occurs when covenant evidence contradicts covenant system.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 15 in full. Verse 7-8 — are you the first man who was born? Or were you brought forth before the hills? Have you listened in the council of Creator? Elifaz's sarcastic challenge: Iyov speaks as if he has privileged covenant access to cosmic covenant truth. The irony — which only the covenant reader knows — is that Iyov's covenant protest is more accurately aligned with the covenant truth of the heavenly council than the friends' confident covenant theology.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 15 in full. Verse 14-16 — what is man that he can be pure? How much less one who is abominable and corrupt, a man who drinks injustice like water. Elifaz moves from the covenant general principle of human covenant impurity to the covenant specific application to Iyov: the covenant general theological principle is legitimate; the covenant specific application to Iyov as one who drinks injustice like water is theologically unsupported slander.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25-26 — because he has stretched out his hand against Creator and defies the Almighty, running stubbornly against him with a thickly bossed shield. Elifaz now directly identifies Iyov as one who defies the Almighty. The covenant friends have moved from covenant gentle correction in the first cycle to covenant direct covenant accusation in the second. The covenant escalation is the covenant sign of covenant frustration: Iyov's refusal to accept their covenant framework requires them to intensify the covenant accusation to maintain the covenant system.

IYOV — CHAPTER 16 Miserable Comforters Are You All — I Also Could Speak as You Do — Creator Has Handed Me Over to the Ungodly — My Face Is Red With Weeping — Even Now My Witness Is in Heaven — O Earth Cover Not My Blood

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 I have heard many such things. Miserable comforters are you all.

3 Shall windy words have an end? Or what provokes you that you answer?

4 I also could speak as you do, if you were in my place. I could join words together against you and shake my head at you.

5 I could strengthen you with my mouth, and the solace of my lips would assuage your pain.

6 If I speak, my pain is not assuaged, and if I forbear, how much of it leaves me?

7 Surely now Creator has worn me out. He has made desolate all my company.

8 And he has shriveled me up, which is a witness against me. And my leanness has risen up against me. It testifies to my face.

9 He has torn me in his wrath and hated me. He has gnashed his teeth at me. My adversary sharpens his eyes against me.

10 Men have gaped at me with their mouth. They have struck me insolently on the cheek. They mass themselves together against me.

11 Creator gives me up to the ungodly and casts me into the hands of the wicked.

12 I was at ease, and he broke me apart. He seized me by the neck and dashed me to pieces. He set me up as his target.

13 His archers surround me. He slashes open my kidneys and does not spare. He pours out my gall on the ground.

14 He breaks me with breach upon breach. He runs upon me like a warrior.

15 I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin and have laid my strength in the dust.

16 My face is red with weeping, and on my eyelids is deep darkness,

17 Although there is no violence in my hands, and my prayer is pure.

18 O earth, cover not my blood, and let my cry find no resting place.

19 Even now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and he who testifies for me is on high.

20 My friends scorn me. My eye pours out tears to Creator,

21 That he would argue the case of a man with Creator, as a son of man does with his neighbor.

22 For when a few years have come I shall go the way from which I shall not return.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Iyov's second response to Elifaz contains one of the most theologically astonishing verses in the book: verse 19 — even now, my witness is in heaven, and he who testifies for me is on high. The man who longs for a covenant arbiter in Chapter 9:33 now declares that he has a covenant witness in heaven. This covenant witness is YHWH himself — the same Creator whom Iyov experiences as his covenant adversary is simultaneously the covenant only covenant witness who can vindicate him.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 16 in full. Verse 19-21 — even now, my witness is in heaven, and he who testifies for me is on high. My friends scorn me. My eye pours out tears to Creator, that he would argue the case of a man with Creator. Iyov appeals through tears to YHWH against YHWH: the covenant man who experiences YHWH as covenant adversary simultaneously experiences YHWH as the covenant only one who can be his covenant advocate. The covenant relationship with YHWH contains both poles within itself.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 16 in full. Verse 4-5 — I also could speak as you do, if you were in my place. I could join words together against you — I could strengthen you with my mouth. Iyov's covenant most damning counter-covenant argument: the friends' comfortable covenant theology is easy from a position of covenant ease. If Iyov were where they stand, he could speak the same words. The covenant test of covenant theology is whether it holds in the darkness, not whether it sounds good in the light.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — although there is no violence in my hands, and my prayer is pure. The covenant center of Iyov's covenant self-defense: amid the description of YHWH's covenant violent covenant assault on Iyov, Iyov's covenant hands are clean and his covenant prayer is pure. The covenant contrast between covenant exterior covenant suffering and covenant interior covenant integrity is the covenant key to the entire book: covenant suffering does not prove covenant interior covenant corruption.

IYOV — CHAPTER 17 My Spirit Is Broken — My Eye Has Grown Dim — Who Will Put Up Security for Me — Creator Has Closed Their Minds to Understanding — Where Then Is My Hope — Will It Go Down to the Bars of Sheol

1 My spirit is broken. My days are extinct. The graveyard is ready for me.

2 Surely there are mockers about me, and my eye dwells on their provocation.

3 Lay down a pledge for me with yourself. Who is there who will put up security for me?

4 Since you have closed their minds to understanding, therefore you will not let them triumph.

5 He informs against friends for a share of the spoil, while the eyes of his children fail.

6 He has made me a byword of the peoples, and I am one before whom men spit.

7 My eye has grown dim from vexation, and all my members are like a shadow.

8 The upright are appalled at this, and the innocent stirs himself up against the godless.

9 Yet the righteous holds to his way, and he who has clean hands grows stronger and stronger.

10 But you, come on again, all of you, and I shall not find a wise man among you.

11 My days are past. My plans are broken off, the desires of my heart.

12 They make night into day. The light, they say, is near to the darkness.

13 If I hope for Sheol as my house, if I make my bed in darkness,

14 If I say to the pit, You are my father, and to the worm, My mother or My sister,

15 Where then is my hope? Who will see my hope?

16 Will it go down to the bars of Sheol? Shall we descend together into the dust?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments. Chapter 17 is the covenant shortest of Iyov's covenant speeches in the second cycle — a covenant compressed covenant lament in which the covenant hope of Chapter 14 seems to have collapsed. Where Chapter 14 ended with the covenant conditional hope of a covenant renewal, Chapter 17 ends with the covenant question: where then is my hope? The covenant oscillation between covenant hope and covenant despair is the covenant authentic covenant rhythm of covenant genuine covenant suffering.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 17 in full. Verse 9 — yet the righteous holds to his way, and he who has clean hands grows stronger and stronger. Within the covenant lament, Iyov articulates the covenant principle that the covenant narrative will ultimately prove true in his own covenant case: the covenant righteous man who holds to his covenant way through covenant suffering emerges covenant stronger. Iyov's covenant theology of covenant integrity is more accurate than the friends' covenant theology of covenant retribution.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 17 in full. Verse 3 — lay down a pledge for me with yourself. Who is there who will put up security for me? Iyov's covenant legal petition: he asks YHWH to be his own covenant guarantor — to put up covenant security on Iyov's behalf against Iyov's own covenant case. The covenant situation is paradoxical: Iyov needs YHWH as his covenant guarantor against YHWH's own covenant judgment of him.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — my spirit is broken. My days are extinct. The graveyard is ready for me. The covenant three-clause opening of the covenant chapter captures the covenant triple exhaustion of Iyov at this covenant point: covenant spiritual covenant depletion, covenant temporal covenant awareness of death, and covenant physical covenant readiness for the grave. The covenant man who declared though he slay me I will hope in him is also the covenant man who says my spirit is broken. Both covenant statements are authentic.

IYOV — CHAPTER 18 Then Bildad the Shuhite Answered — How Long Will You Hunt for Words — The Light of the Wicked Is Put Out — His Roots Dry Up Beneath — His Confidence Is Torn From His Tent — He Is Thrust From Light Into Darkness — This Is the Place of Him Who Knows Not Creator

1 Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:

2 How long will you hunt for words? Consider, and then we will speak.

3 Why are we counted as cattle? Why are we stupid in your sight?

4 You who tear yourself in your anger — shall the earth be forsaken for you, or the rock be removed out of its place?

5 Indeed, the light of the wicked is put out, and the flame of his fire does not shine.

6 The light is dark in his tent, and his lamp above him is put out.

7 His strong steps are shortened, and his own schemes throw him down.

8 For he is cast into a net by his own feet, and he walks on its mesh.

9 A trap seizes him by the heel. A snare lays hold of him.

10 A rope is hidden for him in the ground, a trap for him in the path.

11 Terrors frighten him on every side, and chase him at his heels.

12 His strength is famished, and calamity is ready for his stumbling.

13 It consumes the parts of his skin. The firstborn of death consumes his limbs.

14 He is torn from the tent in which he trusted and is brought to the king of terrors.

15 In his tent dwells that which is none of his. Sulfur is scattered over his habitation.

16 His roots dry up beneath, and his branches wither above.

17 His memory perishes from the earth, and he has no name in the street.

18 He is thrust from light into darkness, and driven out of the world.

19 He has no posterity or progeny among his people, and no survivor where he used to live.

20 Those in the west are appalled at his day, and horror seizes those in the east.

21 Surely such are the dwellings of the unrighteous, such is the place of him who does not know Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments. Bildad's second speech is a covenant catalog of the fate of the covenant wicked — a covenant magnificent covenant poem about covenant doom — with no acknowledgment that the covenant description might not apply to Iyov. The covenant literary quality of the covenant speech is high; the covenant theological application is devastating in its covenant casual cruelty: the entire covenant catalog of covenant wicked covenant fate is a covenant portrait of Iyov's covenant present condition.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 18 in full. Verse 21 — surely such are the dwellings of the unrighteous, such is the place of him who does not know Creator. Bildad's covenant summary: the entire covenant picture he has painted — roots drying up, light extinguished, name perishing, being thrust into darkness — is the covenant place of the one who does not know Creator. Since Iyov is in exactly this condition, the implied covenant conclusion is that Iyov does not know Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 18 in full. Verse 5 — the light of the wicked is put out, and the flame of his fire does not shine. The covenant image of extinguished covenant light is central to Bildad's covenant second speech. The covenant lamp of the covenant wicked is put out — and Iyov's covenant lamp has been extinguished by YHWH's covenant testing. Bildad's covenant imagery applies perfectly to Iyov's covenant external covenant situation — and is utterly wrong about Iyov's covenant internal covenant condition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — he is torn from the tent in which he trusted and is brought to the king of terrors. The covenant king of terrors — melek ballahot — is the covenant most evocative covenant death-image in the second cycle of covenant speeches. Bildad's covenant poetic covenant skill is undeniable; his covenant theological application is covenant condemned by YHWH in Chapter 42:7. The covenant beautiful covenant poem is theologically false when applied to Iyov.

IYOV — CHAPTER 19 How Long Will You Torment Me — Know Then That Creator Has Put Me in the Wrong — My Relatives Have Failed Me — I Am a Foreigner in Their Sight — I Know That My Redeemer Lives — In My Flesh I Shall See Creator

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 How long will you torment me and break me in pieces with words?

3 These ten times you have cast reproach upon me. Are you not ashamed to wrong me?

4 And even if it is true that I have erred, my error remains with myself.

5 If indeed you magnify yourselves against me and make my disgrace an argument against me,

6 Know then that Creator has put me in the wrong and closed his net about me.

7 Behold, I cry out, Violence, but I am not answered. I call for help, but there is no justice.

8 He has walled up my way so that I cannot pass, and he has set darkness upon my paths.

9 He has stripped from me my glory and taken the crown from my head.

10 He breaks me down on every side, and I am gone. He has uprooted my hope like a tree.

11 He has kindled his wrath against me and counts me as one of his adversaries.

12 His troops come on together. They have cast up their siege ramp against me and encamp around my tent.

13 He has put my brothers far from me, and those who knew me are wholly estranged from me.

14 My relatives have failed me, my close friends have forgotten me.

15 The guests in my house and my maidservants count me as a stranger. I have become a foreigner in their eyes.

16 I call to my servant, but he gives me no answer. I must plead with him with my mouth for mercy.

17 My breath is strange to my wife, and I am a stench to the children of my own mother.

18 Even young children despise me. When I rise they talk against me.

19 All my intimate friends abhor me, and those whom I loved have turned against me.

20 My bones stick to my skin and to my flesh, and I have escaped by the skin of my teeth.

21 Have mercy on me, have mercy on me, O you my friends, for the hand of Creator has touched me.

22 Why do you, like Creator, pursue me? Why are you not satisfied with my flesh?

23 Oh that my words were written. Oh that they were inscribed in a book.

24 Oh that with an iron pen and lead they were engraved in the rock forever.

25 For I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth.

26 And after my skin has been thus destroyed, yet in my flesh I shall see Creator,

27 Whom I shall see for myself, and my eyes shall behold, and not another. My heart faints within me.

28 If you say, How we will pursue him, and, The root of the matter is found in him,

29 Be afraid of the sword, for wrath brings the punishment of the sword, that you may know there is a judgment.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 19 is the covenant theological pinnacle of the entire book: verse 25-27 — I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth. And after my skin has been thus destroyed, yet in my flesh I shall see Creator. The covenant man who has been stripped of everything declares the covenant ultimate covenant hope: a living Redeemer who will stand on the earth, and a personal covenant vision of Creator.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 19 in full. Verse 25-27 — for I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth. And after my skin has been thus destroyed, yet in my flesh I shall see Creator, whom I shall see for myself, and my eyes shall behold, and not another. My heart faints within me. The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves this covenant declaration as the covenant central covenant confession of the book: the covenant certainty of the living Redeemer precedes and sustains all covenant human covenant suffering.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 19 in full. Verse 23-24 — oh that my words were written. Oh that they were inscribed in a book. Oh that with an iron pen and lead they were engraved in the rock forever. The covenant irony of the covenant record: Iyov's covenant wish for permanent covenant documentation of his covenant case is granted — his covenant words are inscribed in the covenant book that bears his covenant name and has been preserved for millennia. The covenant book of Iyov is the covenant answer to his covenant prayer.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25 — for I know that my Redeemer lives. The covenant Go'el — the Redeemer — is the same covenant institution invoked in Ruth and Vayikra 25: the nearest covenant male relative who is obligated to redeem the covenant family member in distress. Iyov declares that he has a living covenant Go'el who will stand on the earth and vindicate him. The covenant identity of this covenant Redeemer — within the covenant internal logic of the book — can only be YHWH himself, who is simultaneously Iyov's covenant perceived adversary and covenant ultimate covenant advocate.

IYOV — CHAPTER 20 Then Tzofar the Na'amathite Answered — Do You Not Know This From of Old — The Exulting of the Wicked Is Short — Though His Loftiness Mount Up to the Heavens — He Will Swallow Down Riches and Vomit Them Up — Creator Will Send His Burning Anger

1 Then Tzofar the Na'amathite answered and said:

2 Therefore my thoughts answer me, because of my haste within me.

3 I hear censure that insults me, and out of my understanding a spirit answers me.

4 Do you not know this from of old, since man was placed on earth,

5 That the exulting of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless but for a moment?

6 Though his loftiness mount up to the heavens, and his head reach to the clouds,

7 He will perish forever like his own dung. Those who have seen him will say, Where is he?

8 He will fly away like a dream and not be found. He will be chased away like a vision of the night.

9 The eye that saw him will see him no more, nor will his place any more behold him.

10 His children will seek the favor of the poor, and his hands will give back his wealth.

11 His bones are full of his youthful vigor, but it will lie down with him in the dust.

12 Though evil is sweet in his mouth, though he hides it under his tongue,

13 Though he is loath to let it go and holds it in his mouth,

14 Yet his food is turned in his stomach. It is the venom of cobras within him.

15 He swallows down riches and vomits them up again. Creator casts them out of his belly.

16 He will suck the poison of cobras. The tongue of a viper will kill him.

17 He will not look upon the rivers, the streams flowing with honey and curds.

18 He will give back the fruit of his toil and will not swallow it down. From the profit of his trading he will get no enjoyment.

19 For he has crushed and abandoned the poor. He has seized a house that he did not build.

20 Because he knew no contentment in his belly, he will not let anything in which he delights escape him.

21 There was nothing left after he had eaten. Therefore his prosperity will not endure.

22 In the fullness of his sufficiency he will be in distress. The hand of everyone in misery will come against him.

23 To fill his belly to the full, Creator will send his burning anger against him and rain it upon him into his body.

24 He will flee from an iron weapon. A bronze arrow will strike him through.

25 It is drawn forth and comes out of his body. The glittering point comes out of his gallbladder. Terrors come upon him.

26 Utter darkness is laid up for his treasures. A fire not fanned will devour him. What is left in his tent will be consumed.

27 The heavens will reveal his iniquity, and the earth will rise up against him.

28 The possessions of his house will be carried away, dragged off in the day of Creator's wrath.

29 This is the wicked man's portion from Creator, the heritage decreed for him by Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments. Tzofar's second speech is another elaborate covenant poem about the covenant fate of the covenant wicked — beautiful covenant poetry applied as a covenant weapon against Iyov. The covenant structural problem with Tzofar's covenant argument is the same as Bildad's: Iyov's covenant suffering corresponds perfectly to the covenant fate of the covenant wicked in the covenant poem, but the covenant correspondence does not prove the covenant conclusion.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full. Verse 29 — this is the wicked man's portion from Creator, the heritage decreed for him by Creator. Tzofar's covenant closing statement is the covenant summary indictment: the covenant entire covenant catalog of covenant calamity he has described is the covenant wicked man's heritage. Since Iyov's covenant life matches the covenant catalog, Tzofar's covenant logic requires Iyov to be the covenant wicked man. The covenant syllogism is covenant elegant and covenant wrong.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 20 in full. Verse 4-5 — do you not know this from of old, that the exulting of the wicked is short? Tzofar's appeal to covenant antiquity: the covenant tradition of the fathers — like Bildad — is the covenant authority for his covenant position. The covenant claim is that covenant traditional covenant wisdom has resolved the covenant question of covenant suffering. Iyov's covenant response in Chapter 21 will demolish this covenant appeal with covenant empirical covenant counter-evidence.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — for he has crushed and abandoned the poor. He has seized a house that he did not build. Tzofar adds a specific covenant moral indictment: Iyov has oppressed the poor and stolen covenant property. This covenant specific covenant accusation — like Elifaz's covenant accusations in Chapter 22 — has no covenant evidentiary basis within the covenant narrative. The covenant friends are willing to invent covenant specific covenant sins to support their covenant theological system.

IYOV — CHAPTER 21 Listen Carefully to My Words — Why Do the Wicked Live — Their Houses Are Safe From Fear — Yet They Say to Creator Depart From Us — How Often Is the Lamp of the Wicked Put Out — Their Disaster Is of No Benefit to Them

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 Keep listening to my words, and let this be your comfort.

3 Bear with me, and I will speak, and after I have spoken, mock on.

4 As for me, is my complaint against man? Why should I not be impatient?

5 Look at me and be appalled, and lay your hand over your mouth.

6 When I remember, I am dismayed, and shuddering seizes my flesh.

7 Why do the wicked live, reach old age, and grow mighty in power?

8 Their offspring are established in their presence, and their descendants before their eyes.

9 Their houses are safe from fear, and no rod of Creator is upon them.

10 Their bull breeds without fail. Their cow calves and does not miscarry.

11 They send out their little boys like a flock, and their children dance.

12 They sing to the tambourine and the lyre and rejoice to the sound of the pipe.

13 They spend their days in prosperity, and in peace they go down to Sheol.

14 They say to Creator, Depart from us. We do not desire the knowledge of your ways.

15 What is the Almighty, that we should serve him? And what profit do we get if we pray to him?

16 Behold, is not their prosperity in their hand? The counsel of the wicked is far from me.

17 How often is the lamp of the wicked put out? How often does calamity come upon them? How often does Creator distribute pains in his anger?

18 How often are they like straw before the wind, and like chaff that the storm carries away?

19 You say, Creator stores up their iniquity for their children. Let him pay it out to them, that they may know it.

20 Let their own eyes see their destruction, and let them drink of the wrath of the Almighty.

21 For what do they care for their houses after them, when the number of their months is cut off?

22 Will anyone teach Creator knowledge, seeing that he judges those who are on high?

23 One dies in his full vigor, being wholly at ease and secure,

24 His pails full of milk and the marrow of his bones moist.

25 Another dies in bitterness of soul, never having tasted of prosperity.

26 They lie down alike in the dust, and the worms cover them.

27 Behold, I know your thoughts and your schemes to wrong me.

28 For you say, Where is the house of the prince? Where is the tent in which the wicked lived?

29 Have you not asked those who travel the roads, and do you not accept their testimony

30 That the evil man is spared in the day of calamity, that he is rescued in the day of wrath?

31 Who declares his way to his face, and who repays him for what he has done?

32 When he is carried to the grave, watch is kept over his tomb.

33 The clods of the valley are sweet to him. All mankind follows after him, and those who go before him are innumerable.

34 How then will you comfort me with empty nothings? There is nothing left of your answers but falsehood.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 21 is Iyov's definitive empirical refutation of the friends' covenant theology of covenant retribution: the wicked live, reach old age, grow mighty, their houses are safe, their children flourish — and they die in peace. The covenant observation is honest and devastating: the covenant system of covenant immediate retribution does not operate as the friends claim. Iyov's covenant counter-argument is not covenant theological speculation but covenant empirical covenant observation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full. Verse 7 — why do the wicked live, reach old age, and grow mighty in power? The covenant question that the friends' covenant theology cannot answer: if covenant wickedness automatically produces covenant suffering, why do the covenant wicked flourish? Iyov's covenant honest covenant empirical challenge is more truthful about covenant experienced reality than the friends' covenant theoretical covenant system.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 21 in full. Verse 34 — how then will you comfort me with empty nothings? There is nothing left of your answers but falsehood. Iyov's covenant summary of the second cycle of the friends' covenant speeches: empty nothings — hevel — and falsehood — ma'al. The covenant friends who came to comfort have failed at covenant comfort and failed at covenant truth. Their covenant comfort is empty; their covenant theology is false when applied to Iyov's covenant case.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14-15 — they say to Creator, Depart from us. We do not desire the knowledge of your ways. What is the Almighty, that we should serve him? And what profit do we get if we pray to him? Iyov quotes the covenant speech of the covenant wicked — the covenant people who explicitly reject YHWH — and points out that they prosper anyway. The covenant Adversary's question in Chapter 1:9 — does Iyov fear Creator for no reason — is here turned against the friends' covenant system: the covenant wicked do not fear Creator and receive no punishment.

THE THIRD CYCLE — CHAPTERS 22 THROUGH 28 — THE FRIENDS REACH THEIR LIMIT

IYOV — CHAPTER 22 Then Elifaz the Temanite Answered — Can a Man Be Profitable to Creator — Is Not Your Evil Abundant — For You Have Stripped the Naked of Their Clothing — Therefore Snares Are All Around You — Agree With Creator and Be at Peace

1 Then Elifaz the Temanite answered and said:

2 Can a man be profitable to Creator? Surely he who is wise is profitable to himself.

3 Is it any pleasure to the Almighty if you are in the right, or is it gain to him if you make your ways blameless?

4 Is it for your fear of him that he reproves you and enters into judgment with you?

5 Is not your evil abundant? There is no end to your iniquities.

6 For you have exacted pledges of your brothers for nothing and stripped the naked of their clothing.

7 You have given no water to the weary to drink, and you have withheld bread from the hungry.

8 The man with power possessed the land, and the favored man lived in it.

9 You have sent widows away empty, and the arms of the fatherless were crushed.

10 Therefore snares are all around you, and sudden terror overwhelms you,

11 Or darkness, so that you cannot see, and a flood of water covers you.

12 Is not Creator high in the heavens? See the highest stars, how lofty they are.

13 But you say, What does Creator know? Can he judge through the deep darkness?

14 Thick clouds veil him, so that he does not see, and he walks on the vault of heaven.

15 Will you keep to the old way that wicked men have trod?

16 They were snatched away before their time. Their foundation was washed away.

17 They said to Creator, Depart from us, and What can the Almighty do to us?

18 Yet he filled their houses with good things — but the counsel of the wicked is far from me.

19 The righteous see it and are glad. The innocent one mocks at them,

20 Saying, Surely our adversaries are cut off, and what they left the fire has consumed.

21 Agree with Creator and be at peace. Thereby good will come to you.

22 Receive instruction from his mouth, and lay up his words in your heart.

23 If you return to the Almighty you will be built up. If you remove injustice far from your tents,

24 If you lay gold in the dust, and gold of Ofir among the stones of the torrent-bed,

25 Then the Almighty will be your gold and your precious silver.

26 For then you will delight yourself in the Almighty and lift up your face to Creator.

27 You will make your prayer to him, and he will hear you, and you will pay your vows.

28 You will decide on a matter, and it will be established for you, and light will shine on your ways.

29 For when they are humbled you say, It is because of pride. But he saves the lowly.

30 He delivers even the one who is not innocent, who will be delivered through the cleanness of your hands.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Elifaz's third and final speech abandons covenant gentle suggestion and invents specific covenant sins: verse 6-9 — you have stripped the naked, withheld bread from the hungry, sent widows away empty, crushed the fatherless. These are covenant specific covenant moral indictments with no covenant evidentiary basis in the covenant narrative. The covenant friends have arrived at covenant slander in their attempt to maintain their covenant theological system against Iyov's covenant challenge.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 22 in full. Verse 21-23 — agree with Creator and be at peace. Thereby good will come to you. Receive instruction from his mouth. If you return to the Almighty you will be built up. The covenant invitation at the center of Elifaz's third speech is beautiful — it would be true counsel for a covenant man who had sinned. The covenant problem: Iyov has not committed the covenant sins Elifaz has just invented. The covenant counsel is correct in form and wrong in application.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 22 in full. Verse 6-9 — the specific covenant sins that Elifaz invents: stripped the naked, withheld water and bread, sent widows away empty, crushed the arms of the fatherless. These will be directly and precisely refuted by Iyov in Chapter 31 — where Iyov swears an oath of covenant innocence covering exactly these categories. The covenant specificity of Elifaz's covenant accusations is the covenant specificity that Iyov will covenant specifically deny.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-4 — can a man be profitable to Creator? Is it any pleasure to the Almighty if you are in the right, or is it gain to him if you make your ways blameless? Elifaz's theology of divine detachment: YHWH's actions toward human beings are not based on what humans actually do because YHWH has no stake in human righteousness or wickedness. This covenant theology, while intending to magnify YHWH's transcendence, actually undermines the entire covenant framework of the book — and contradicts YHWH's own statement in Chapter 1:8 about his servant Iyov.

IYOV — CHAPTER 23 Oh That I Knew Where I Might Find Him — I Would Lay My Case Before Him — I Would Know What He Would Answer Me — Behold I Go Forward But He Is Not There — He Knows the Way That I Take — When He Has Tried Me I Shall Come Out as Gold

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 Today also my complaint is bitter. My hand is heavy on account of my groaning.

3 Oh that I knew where I might find him, that I might come even to his seat.

4 I would lay my case before him and fill my mouth with arguments.

5 I would know what he would answer me and understand what he would say to me.

6 Would he contend with me in the greatness of his power? No, he would pay attention to me.

7 There an upright man could argue with him, and I would be acquitted forever by my judge.

8 Behold, I go forward, but he is not there, and backward, but I do not perceive him.

9 On the left hand when he is working, I do not behold him. He turns to the right hand, but I do not see him.

10 But he knows the way that I take. When he has tried me, I shall come out as gold.

11 My foot has held fast to his steps. I have kept his way and have not turned aside.

12 I have not departed from the commandment of his lips. I have treasured the words of his mouth more than my portion of food.

13 But he is unchangeable, and who can turn him back? What he desires, that he does.

14 For he will complete what he appoints for me, and many such things are in his mind.

15 Therefore I am terrified at his presence. When I consider, I am in dread of him.

16 Creator has made my heart faint. The Almighty has terrified me.

17 Yet I am not silenced because of the darkness, nor because thick darkness covers my face.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 23 is Iyov's most poignant covenant speech: he longs to find YHWH and lay his case before him — not to escape from YHWH but to encounter YHWH. The covenant man whose covenant suffering has come from YHWH most deeply desires nothing other than YHWH's covenant presence and YHWH's covenant answer. The covenant longing for YHWH in the context of covenant suffering from YHWH is the covenant paradox at the heart of covenant authentic covenant faith.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 23 in full. Verse 10 — but he knows the way that I take. When he has tried me, I shall come out as gold. The covenant declaration embedded within the covenant search for YHWH: Iyov cannot find YHWH — verses 8-9 — but he knows that YHWH sees him. The covenant asymmetry is the covenant human condition: we cannot find YHWH but YHWH knows the covenant way we take. The covenant testing that seems covenant invisible produces covenant gold.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 23 in full. Verse 12 — I have not departed from the commandment of his lips. I have treasured the words of his mouth more than my portion of food. Iyov's covenant oath of covenant Torah faithfulness: the covenant man accused by the friends of covenant sin and apostasy declares that he has treasured YHWH's covenant words more than covenant food. This covenant declaration will be confirmed by YHWH's covenant verdict in Chapter 42:7.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — oh that I knew where I might find him, that I might come even to his seat. I would lay my case before him and fill my mouth with arguments. Iyov's covenant desire to find YHWH is the covenant opposite of the covenant wicked's covenant statement in Chapter 21:14 — depart from us. The covenant man who suffers most intensely from his covenant relationship with YHWH is also the covenant man who most intensely seeks YHWH. Covenant suffering does not produce covenant flight from YHWH in the covenant authentic covenant believer.

IYOV — CHAPTER 24 Why Are Not Times of Judgment Kept by the Almighty — The Wicked Remove Landmarks — The Murderer Rises in the Dark — They Are Exalted a Little While and Then Are Gone — Yet Creator Charges No One With Wrong

1 Why are not times of judgment kept by the Almighty, and why do those who know him never see his days?

2 Some move landmarks. They seize flocks and pasture them.

3 They drive away the donkey of the fatherless. They take the widow's ox for a pledge.

4 They thrust the poor off the road. The poor of the earth all hide themselves.

5 Behold, like wild donkeys in the desert, the poor go out to their toil, seeking game. The wilderness yields them food for their children.

6 They gather their fodder in the field, and they glean the vineyard of the wicked man.

7 They lie all night naked, without clothing, and have no covering in the cold.

8 They are wet with the showers of the mountains and cling to the rock for lack of shelter.

9 There are those who snatch the fatherless child from the breast, and they take a pledge against the poor.

10 They go about naked, without clothing. Hungry, they carry the sheaves.

11 Among the olive rows of the wicked they make oil. They tread the winepresses, but suffer thirst.

12 From out of the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries for help. Yet Creator charges no one with wrong.

13 There are those who rebel against the light, who are not acquainted with its ways, and do not stay in its paths.

14 The murderer rises before it is light, that he may kill the poor and needy, and in the night he is like a thief.

15 The eye of the adulterer also waits for the twilight, saying, No eye will see me, and he veils his face.

16 In the dark they dig through houses. By day they shut themselves up. They do not know the light.

17 For deep darkness is morning to all of them. For they are friends with the terrors of deep darkness.

18 You say, Swift are they on the face of the waters. Their portion is cursed in the land. No treader turns toward their vineyards.

19 Drought and heat snatch away the snow waters. So does Sheol those who have sinned.

20 The womb forgets them. The worm finds them sweet. They are no longer remembered, so wickedness is broken like a tree.

21 They wrong the barren, childless woman, and do no good to the widow.

22 Yet Creator drags away the mighty by his power. He rises up, but he has no assurance of life.

23 He gives them security, and they are supported, and his eyes are upon their ways.

24 They are exalted a little while, and then are gone. They are brought low and gathered up like all others. They are cut off like the heads of grain.

25 If it is not so, who will prove me a liar and show that there is nothing in what I say?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 24 is Iyov's extended covenant lament over covenant unanswered covenant injustice: the wicked oppress the poor — move landmarks, seize flocks, exploit the fatherless and widow — and YHWH charges no one with wrong. Iyov's covenant social covenant concern is not covenant abstract: he catalogs specific covenant social covenant crimes with covenant journalistic covenant precision.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 24 in full. Verse 12 — from out of the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries for help. Yet Creator charges no one with wrong. The covenant indictment of covenant divine covenant inaction: Iyov does not deny YHWH's existence or covenant power — he protests YHWH's apparent covenant indifference to the covenant cries of the covenant dying and the covenant wounded. The covenant protest is the covenant authentic cry of the covenant prophetic tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 24 in full. Verse 25 — if it is not so, who will prove me a liar and show that there is nothing in what I say? Iyov's covenant challenge: if his covenant empirical covenant observations about covenant unpunished covenant wickedness are wrong, let anyone prove him wrong with covenant evidence. The covenant challenge is not covenant arrogance but covenant confidence in covenant honest covenant observation. No one in the covenant dialogue answers the covenant challenge.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — why are not times of judgment kept by the Almighty, and why do those who know him never see his days? The covenant opening question of Chapter 24 is the covenant theodicy question that the covenant entire book revolves around: why does YHWH not schedule covenant visible covenant judgment? The covenant question is not covenant atheism — it assumes YHWH's existence and YHWH's capacity for covenant judgment — but protests the covenant inexplicable covenant delay of covenant visible covenant justice.

IYOV — CHAPTER 25 Then Bildad the Shuhite Answered — Dominion and Fear Are With Creator — How Then Can Man Be in the Right Before Creator — Man Who Is a Maggot — The Son of Man Who Is a Worm

1 Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:

2 Dominion and fear are with Creator. He makes peace in his high heaven.

3 Is there any number to his armies? Upon whom does his light not arise?

4 How then can man be in the right before Creator? How can he who is born of woman be pure?

5 Behold, even the moon is not bright, and the stars are not pure in his eyes.

6 How much less man, who is a maggot, and the son of man, who is a worm.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Bildad's third speech is the shortest speech in the entire covenant dialogue — six verses — and the covenant compiler's comment on the covenant exhaustion of the friends' covenant theological position. Having nothing new to say, Bildad simply restates the covenant principle of human unworthiness before Creator. The brevity is the covenant sign that the covenant friends' covenant system has run out of covenant arguments while Iyov's covenant testimony continues.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 25 in full. Verse 6 — how much less man, who is a maggot, and the son of man, who is a worm. Bildad's covenant final statement about humanity reduces covenant human beings to covenant vermin. The covenant theological position that begins with covenant human unworthiness ends with covenant human worthlessness. The covenant friends who set out to comfort a covenant man in covenant suffering conclude by calling him a worm.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 25 in full. Verse 4 — how then can man be in the right before Creator? The question that Elifaz's night-vision asked in Chapter 4:17 is recycled by Bildad. The covenant friends' covenant second and third cycles increasingly repeat covenant first-cycle themes with less nuance. The covenant theological system is not developing — it is contracting into its foundational axioms.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The brevity of Chapter 25 — the shortest chapter in the covenant dialogue — is itself a theological statement. The friends have nothing left to say because their covenant system cannot accommodate Iyov's covenant evidence. Six verses of covenant cosmological covenant humility followed by covenant human-as-worm is the covenant best that the covenant exhausted covenant system can produce. Iyov's covenant response in Chapters 26-31 will be the covenant longest and most comprehensive covenant speech in the entire covenant dialogue.

IYOV — CHAPTER 26 How You Have Helped Him Who Has No Power — The Dead Tremble Below the Waters — He Stretches Out the North Over the Void — He Binds Up the Waters in His Thick Clouds — Behold These Are But the Outskirts of His Ways

1 Then Iyov answered and said:

2 How you have helped him who has no power. How you have saved the arm that has no strength.

3 How you have counseled him who has no wisdom, and plentifully declared sound knowledge.

4 With whose help have you uttered words, and whose breath has come out from you?

5 The dead tremble under the waters and their inhabitants.

6 Sheol is naked before Creator, and Abaddon has no covering.

7 He stretches out the north over the void and hangs the earth on nothing.

8 He binds up the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud is not split open under them.

9 He covers the face of the full moon and spreads over it his cloud.

10 He has inscribed a circle on the face of the waters at the boundary between light and darkness.

11 The pillars of heaven tremble and are astounded at his rebuke.

12 By his power he stilled the sea. By his understanding he shattered Rahab.

13 By his wind the heavens were made fair. His hand pierced the fleeing serpent.

14 Behold, these are but the outskirts of his ways, and how small a whisper do we hear of him. But the thunder of his power who can understand?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Iyov's response to Bildad's short speech opens with covenant irony — how you have helped him who has no power — and then delivers a covenant hymn to YHWH's covenant creative and covenant cosmic covenant power that surpasses anything the friends have said. Verse 7 — he hangs the earth on nothing — is one of the most remarkable covenant cosmological covenant observations in the ancient covenant world: the earth suspended in the void without physical covenant support.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 26 in full. Verse 14 — behold, these are but the outskirts of his ways, and how small a whisper do we hear of him. But the thunder of his power who can understand? The covenant epistemological covenant humility of Iyov's covenant hymn: everything that can be described about YHWH's covenant cosmic covenant power is only the outermost edge of YHWH's covenant reality. The covenant full thunder of YHWH's covenant power is beyond covenant human covenant comprehension.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 26 in full. Verse 7 — he stretches out the north over the void and hangs the earth on nothing. The covenant ancient covenant cosmological covenant observation that the earth hangs suspended without visible covenant support — on nothing — is the covenant most striking physical covenant observation in the Iyov covenant text. The covenant man of covenant suffering has not lost covenant wonder at the covenant creation order.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-4 — how you have helped him who has no power. With whose help have you uttered words, and whose breath has come out from you? Iyov's devastating covenant sarcasm to Bildad: the six-verse speech about YHWH's covenant transcendence and human covenant unworthiness was no help. Iyov does not need to be told that YHWH is great and humanity is small — he knows. What he needs is covenant authentic covenant engagement with his covenant actual covenant situation, which the friends' covenant theology cannot provide.

IYOV — CHAPTER 27 As Creator Lives Who Has Taken Away My Right — Far Be It From Me to Say You Are Right — I Hold Fast My Righteousness — What Is the Hope of the Godless — Creator Will Destroy Them — This Is the Portion of a Wicked Man

1 And Iyov again took up his discourse and said:

2 As Creator lives, who has taken away my right, and the Almighty, who has made my soul bitter,

3 As long as my breath is in me, and the spirit of Creator is in my nostrils,

4 My lips will not speak falsehood, and my tongue will not utter deceit.

5 Far be it from me to say that you are right. Till I die I will not put away my integrity from me.

6 I hold fast my righteousness and will not let it go. My heart does not reproach me for any of my days.

7 Let my enemy be as the wicked, and let him who rises up against me be as the unrighteous.

8 For what is the hope of the godless when Creator cuts him off, when Creator takes away his life?

9 Will Creator hear his cry when distress comes upon him?

10 Will he take delight in the Almighty? Will he call upon Creator at all times?

11 I will teach you concerning the hand of Creator. What is with the Almighty I will not conceal.

12 Behold, all of you have seen it yourselves. Why then have you become altogether vain?

13 This is the portion of a wicked man with Creator, and the heritage that oppressors receive from the Almighty:

14 If his children are multiplied, it is for the sword, and his descendants have not enough bread.

15 Those who survive him the pestilence buries, and his widows do not weep.

16 Though he pile up silver like dust, and pile up clothing like clay,

17 He may pile it up, but the righteous will wear it, and the innocent will divide the silver.

18 He builds his house like a moth's, like a booth that a watchman makes.

19 He goes to bed rich, but will do so no more. He opens his eyes, and his wealth is gone.

20 Terrors overtake him like a flood. In the night a whirlwind carries him off.

21 The east wind lifts him up and he is gone. It sweeps him out of his place.

22 It hurls itself against him without pity. He flees from its power in headlong flight.

23 It claps its hands at him and hisses at him from its place.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 27 is Iyov's covenant great oath of covenant integrity: as Creator lives — verse 2 — I will not put away my integrity — verse 5. The covenant oath by the name of YHWH is the covenant most formal covenant commitment available in the covenant world: Iyov swears by the very Creator who has made his soul bitter that he will maintain his covenant integrity. The covenant oath is simultaneously the covenant protest and the covenant confession.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 27 in full. Verse 5-6 — far be it from me to say that you are right. Till I die I will not put away my integrity from me. I hold fast my righteousness and will not let it go. My heart does not reproach me for any of my days. Iyov's covenant declaration of covenant heart-innocence: his covenant interior covenant self-knowledge covenant confirms his covenant external covenant claim. The covenant heart that does not reproach him is the covenant internal covenant witness to the covenant external covenant testimony.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 27 in full. Verse 2 — as Creator lives, who has taken away my right, and the Almighty, who has made my soul bitter. The covenant remarkable structure of the oath: Iyov swears by the very Creator he accuses of taking away his right and making his soul bitter. The covenant man who is in covenant dispute with YHWH still invokes YHWH as the ultimate covenant authority. YHWH is simultaneously Iyov's covenant adversary and Iyov's covenant ultimate covenant reference point.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13-23 — This is the portion of a wicked man with Creator — the catalog of the wicked man's fate. The covenant irony: Iyov, who has spent chapters refuting the friends' covenant theology of retribution, here delivers the most thorough covenant description of the covenant wicked man's covenant fate in the covenant dialogue. Iyov does not deny that the covenant wicked are punished — he denies that this covenant punishment is the covenant explanation of his own covenant suffering.

IYOV — CHAPTER 28 Surely There Is a Mine for Silver — But Where Shall Wisdom Be Found — Man Does Not Know Its Worth — It Is Not Found in the Land of the Living — Creator Understands the Way to It — Behold the Fear of the Creator That Is Wisdom

1 Surely there is a mine for silver, and a place for gold that they refine.

2 Iron is taken out of the earth, and copper is smelted from the ore.

3 Man puts an end to darkness and searches out to the farthest limit the ore in gloom and deep darkness.

4 He opens shafts in a valley away from where anyone lives. They are forgotten by travelers. They hang in the air, far away from mankind. They swing to and fro.

5 As for the earth, out of it comes bread, but underneath it is turned up as by fire.

6 Its stones are the place of sapphires, and it has dust of gold.

7 That path no bird of prey knows, and the falcon's eye has not seen it.

8 The proud beasts have not trodden it. The lion has not passed over it.

9 Man puts his hand to the flinty rock and overturns mountains by the roots.

10 He cuts out channels in the rocks, and his eye sees every precious thing.

11 He dams up the streams so that they do not trickle, and the thing that is hidden he brings out to light.

12 But where shall wisdom be found? And where is the place of understanding?

13 Man does not know its worth, and it is not found in the land of the living.

14 The deep says, It is not in me, and the sea says, It is not with me.

15 It cannot be bought for gold, and silver cannot be weighed as its price.

16 It cannot be valued in the gold of Ofir, in precious onyx or sapphire.

17 Gold and glass cannot equal it, nor can it be exchanged for jewels of fine gold.

18 No mention shall be made of coral or of crystal. The price of wisdom is above pearls.

19 The topaz of Kush cannot equal it, and it cannot be valued in pure gold.

20 From where, then, does wisdom come? And where is the place of understanding?

21 It is hidden from the eyes of all living and concealed from the birds of the air.

22 Abaddon and Death say, We have heard a rumor of it with our ears.

23 Creator understands the way to it, and he knows its place.

24 For he looks to the ends of the earth and sees everything under the heavens.

25 When he gave to the wind its weight and apportioned the waters by measure,

26 When he made a decree for the rain and a way for the lightning of the thunder,

27 Then he saw it and declared it. He established it, and searched it out.

28 And he said to man, Behold, the fear of the Creator, that is wisdom, and to turn away from evil is understanding.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text of the Wisdom Hymn of Chapter 28. This chapter is the theological covenant center of the book before YHWH's covenant answer: human beings can mine silver and gold and precious stones from the earth — they can reach the most hidden covenant places in the covenant natural order — but they cannot find covenant wisdom. Only Creator knows the place of covenant wisdom. The covenant definition of covenant wisdom — verse 28 — is the covenant answer that the entire covenant dialogue has been approaching: the fear of Creator, that is wisdom.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 28 in full. Verse 28 — and he said to man, Behold, the fear of the Creator, that is wisdom, and to turn away from evil is understanding. The covenant conclusion of the covenant Wisdom Hymn is the covenant refutation of the friends' covenant position: the friends claim to possess covenant wisdom through their covenant theological system. But the covenant wisdom that YHWH reveals is not a covenant theological system — it is covenant fear of Creator and covenant turning from evil. Iyov, who maintains both, has covenant wisdom.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 28 in full. The Wisdom Hymn of Chapter 28 stands apart from the covenant dialogue as a covenant meditative covenant interlude: it is not clearly assigned to any covenant speaker and functions as the covenant narrative's covenant theological pivot point before YHWH's covenant speech. The covenant question — where shall wisdom be found? — is the covenant question that YHWH's covenant speech from the whirlwind will answer in a covenant unexpected way.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — but where shall wisdom be found? And where is the place of understanding? The covenant Wisdom Hymn's central question is the covenant structural question of the entire covenant book: the friends claim to possess wisdom through their covenant theological tradition; Iyov claims covenant insight through covenant honest covenant experience; the covenant Wisdom Hymn declares that covenant wisdom is not available to any human covenant source. Only Creator knows its location. The covenant answer — the fear of Creator — is the covenant posture that Iyov has maintained throughout the covenant dialogue.

END OF PART TWO — CHAPTERS 15 THROUGH 28

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

I KNOW THAT MY REDEEMER LIVES

Iyov 19:25
